title: Macramé feather set
subtitle: Three knotted feathers in graduated sizes on 3 mm cotton cord
excerpt: A set of three macramé feathers in small (12 cm), medium (18 cm), and large (25 cm) sizes, each made on a short horizontal mount cord. Lark's head mounts attach the barb cords; diagonal double half-hitch rows shape the feather; the fringe is combed open and trimmed to the outline after knotting.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. The macramé feather is a contemporary form that emerged from the mid-20th-century macramé revival; it uses the lark's head mount and double half-hitch barb structure documented across that tradition. Graumont and Hensel, Encyclopedia of Knots and Fancy Rope Work (1939), Cornell Maritime Press, covers the underlying knot structures. The combed-open fringe finish is standard in modern macramé guild practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-loop mounting knot used to attach a folded cord to a ring, dowel, or holding cord. The cord is folded in half, the loop is passed over the mount from front to back, and both tails are pulled through the loop. The result is a secure mount with two working ends hanging below. The lark's head is the standard starting knot for macramé panels and feathers.

A two-wrap knot in which a working cord wraps twice around a filler or holding cord, producing a firm knot that locks in place. Used in rows to create solid horizontal or diagonal bars. On a diagonal holding cord the double half-hitches line up to form the angled spine of a macramé feather barb.

A cord held taut while other working cords knot around it. In a row of double half-hitches the holding cord determines the row direction: held horizontally it produces a horizontal bar, held diagonally it produces a diagonal bar. The holding cord itself does not form a knot; it receives the wraps.

The unworked cord tails left below or beyond the final knot of a macramé piece, trimmed to length and combed or left plain. On a macramé feather the fringe cords form the barbs: combed open they separate into fine strands that are then trimmed to the feather silhouette.

content.text: Each feather in this set is built from short cords mounted with
lark's head knots
on a central spine cord, then shaped with diagonal
double half-hitch
rows and a combed
fringe finish

trimmed to the feather outline. The three sizes are small at roughly 12 cm long, medium at roughly 18 cm, and large at roughly 25 cm. Use 3 mm natural cotton macramé cord for a soft finish that combs well; tighter-twist cord combs into a finer strand while loosely-twisted cord gives a fluffier barb. The cord-cut breakdown is below in the method steps for each size.
What you need

items.name: 3 mm cotton macramé cord, natural
items.qty: About 6 m for the full set of three
items.substitution: Single-strand or 3-ply twisted cord both comb well; braided cord combs into fine strands rather than a fluffy fringe

items.name: Macramé fringe comb or stiff brush

content.text: The knots, step by step
Each feather uses two knot types. The lark's head mounts the barb cords to the spine. The double half-hitch on a diagonal
holding cord
shapes the two angled barb rows that define the feather form.

Lark's head: fold a barb cord in half and lay the fold over the spine cord from front to back. Pull both tails through the loop and tighten snugly against the spine. Every barb cord is mounted this way, producing two working ends per cord.

definition.caption: Lark's head: fold barb cord in half, loop over the spine cord, pull both tails through.

content.text: Double half-hitch on a diagonal holding cord: hold the outermost mounted cord (the one furthest from the spine tip) at a shallow diagonal angle pointing toward the spine tip. Each of the other hanging barb cords wraps twice around this diagonal holding cord in turn, working inward toward the spine. After all cords have wrapped, the holding cord has become a firm diagonal rib running from the outermost barb cord toward the spine tip. Repeat the same operation from the other side using the outermost cord on that side as the diagonal holding cord. The two diagonal ribs create the characteristic angled barb structure of a feather.

definition.caption: Double half-hitch: each working cord wraps twice around the diagonal holding cord to form a firm barb rib.

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the cords for each feather

For the small feather (12 cm finished): cut one spine cord at 40 cm and ten barb cords at 20 cm each. For the medium feather (18 cm finished): cut one spine cord at 55 cm and fourteen barb cords at 28 cm each. For the large feather (25 cm finished): cut one spine cord at 70 cm and eighteen barb cords at 38 cm each.
Cut all cords cleanly with sharp scissors. A clean cut prevents fraying before the knots are tied. Keep each size grouped separately on the board.
Step 2: Set up the spine and mount the barb cords

Pin the spine cord horizontally to the macramé board, stretched taut and secured at both ends with T-pins. The spine serves as the mounting line. Take each barb cord in turn, fold it in half, and mount it to the spine with a lark's head knot, working from left to right. For the small feather mount ten barb cords; for the medium fourteen; for the large eighteen. Pack the lark's head knots closely together so they sit touching side by side with no gap between them.

After all barb cords are mounted you will have a double row of hanging cord ends below the spine: the working ends of every barb cord, 20 per small feather, 28 per medium, and 36 per large. The spine cord extends beyond the lark's head knots at each end; these extension lengths become the quill tip (narrow end) and the hanging loop (wide end) once the feather is shaped.
Step 3: Tie the diagonal double half-hitch rows

Unpin the right end of the spine cord from the board. Take the outermost right-hand barb end (the right tail of the rightmost barb cord) and hold it diagonally angled down toward the left at roughly 30 to 40 degrees. This is the right-side holding cord for the first barb row.

Working from right to left, take each of the remaining hanging barb ends on the right half of the piece and tie a double half-hitch around the diagonal holding cord, moving toward the spine tip. Hold the diagonal cord firmly at its angle with your non-dominant hand; each wrap sits firmly against the previous one as you work inward. When you reach the spine, bring the holding cord behind the spine cord and pin it temporarily to hold the angle. This completes the right-side barb row.

Repeat on the left side: take the outermost left-hand barb end, angle it diagonally down toward the right, and work each left-side barb cord around it with a double half-hitch from left to right toward the spine. The two diagonal rows should meet near the spine tip, producing a V-shape that mirrors the barb structure of a bird feather.

For the medium and large feathers, tie a second set of diagonal rows below the first. Use the outermost barb ends again as the holding cords, angled at a slightly shallower angle. Two rows of barb ribs on each side give the larger feathers a rounder outline.
Step 4: Comb and trim to the feather shape

The trim sequence is: comb first, then trim. Do not trim before combing. Combing untwists the cord ends into loose fibre strands; once the strands are open the feather outline is visible and the trim is accurate. Trimming tightly-twisted cord ends before combing gives an uneven cut because the twist causes the cord to spring when the tension releases.

Hold the feather flat on the board and work the fringe comb through each cord end from the tip upward, using short strokes. Each cord will separate into fine strands. After all ends are combed, the feather has a full, soft outline but the edges are uneven. Use sharp scissors to trim the outline to a feather shape: narrower at the quill tip, widest at the midpoint of the body, tapering to a point at the lower tip. Hold the scissors at a shallow angle to the cord plane rather than cutting straight across, which gives a softer edge.
Repeat for all three feathers. The graduated sizes read best when they are trimmed to the same relative proportions: each feather roughly three times as long as it is wide at the broadest point.
Variations

Dyeing the barb cords in gradients before knotting gives the feather a colour-wash effect. Dip-dye the lower half of each barb cord bundle into a cool plant-dye bath or fabric dye (following the dye's instructions for cotton) before mounting, so the colour fades from the tip upward. Allow the cord to dry fully before knotting.

Two cord colours in alternating lark's head mounts, one light and one dark cord per pair, give a striped feather after combing. The two colours separate into their own strand groups when combed, producing a clear stripe.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The double half-hitch rows do not form a clear diagonal rib; the knots look loose or sliding
items.cause: The holding cord angle was not maintained consistently while the working cords were wrapping, or the wraps were not pulled firmly enough after each one
items.fix: Pin the holding cord at its diagonal angle to the board at both ends before wrapping any cords around it. Hold the pin in place with one hand and wrap each cord with the other. Pull each double half-hitch snug before moving to the next cord.

items.problem: The feather trimmed to an uneven outline with visible cord stumps rather than a clean edge
items.cause: The cord ends were trimmed before combing, so the twist in the cord caused springing that misaligned the cut length
items.fix: Undo the trim as far as possible, comb the remaining ends open, and re-trim. For future pieces, always comb before trimming.

items.problem: The feather body is too narrow or the barbs are sparse
items.cause: Too few barb cords were mounted, or the lark's head knots were spaced too far apart on the spine
items.fix: Pack the lark's head knots tightly side by side along the spine. For a feather with a full, bushy barb, use one barb cord per 3 to 4 mm of spine length. Mounting more cords and trimming generously is better than a sparse mount.
